article_meta.title: Defense Coast Guard denies report it won't classify swastikas, nooses as hate symbols by Filip Timotija 11/20/25 03:23 P

reasoning: The model systematically flags quoted/attributed source language as bias, violating the attribution rule. Items 1-3 all flag language from DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin or DHS statements — these are quoted text inside quotation marks, clearly attributed to named sources. The model should never flag these as bias. Item 4 flags a paraphrase of Coast Guard policy as 'Omission of Source Attribution,' which is questionable — the article does cite the Washington Post's obtained documents and the Coast Guard's own published policy. Meanwhile, the model misses potential author-level bias such as the parenthetical explanation 'widely recognized as a symbol of the German Nazi Party and linked to the killing of millions of Jews' which could be seen as editorializing/contextual spin, though this is arguably neutral context. The article is largely a straight news report with heavy attribution, so the correct output would likely be [] or nearly empty. The model's output is dominated by attribution rule violations.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: Items 1, 2, and 3 all flag language inside quotation marks attributed to DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin or the DHS. These are clear attribution rule violations — the model flags quoted source language as if it were author bias. This is the most common failure pattern described in the calibration examples.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: All four flagged items are either attribution rule violations (items 1-3 flag quoted source language) or questionable (item 4 — the article does attribute the policy content to the Coast Guard and Washington Post). None represent genuine author-level bias.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: This article is largely a straight news report with heavy attribution. There may be minor author-level bias in the contextual parenthetical about swastikas being 'widely recognized as a symbol of the German Nazi Party and linked to the killing of millions of Jews' (potentially editorializing), but this is arguably standard context. The correct output is likely [] or close to it, so missing this is not a severe failure.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: Since the flagged items are mostly attribution rule violations and shouldn't have been flagged at all, the type labels are somewhat moot. However, if we consider the labels applied to the quoted text, 'Mudslinging/Ad Hominem' for McLaughlin's quote and 'Sensationalism/Emotionalism' for the DHS response are at least defensible characterizations of the source language itself — they're just incorrectly attributed to the author.
